meta.note: Jordan + UAE + Qatar sweep, published books. Deduped: The Satanic Verses (id 6) and Ali Dashti's 23 Years (id 160) already existed -> Jordan ban-adds. Jordan's 2010 NPR/Foreign Policy 'most-requested banned books' are recorded historical because Jordan reformed/lifted many bans around 2011 (exact ban years uncertain; proxy used, noted). Qatar's The Gruffalo held (clearance-bottleneck, not an articulated ban). Gulf title-level records are thin; only solidly-sourced titles returned.

entries.title: The Satanic Verses
entries.description_ban: Barred in Jordan as blasphemous against Islam following Khomeini's fatwa; named in 2010 as the most-requested banned book in the country. Adds a Jordan ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Jordan ban; documented by NPR (citing Foreign Policy).

entries.title: 23 Years
entries.description_ban: A study of the prophetic career of Muhammad that questions ascribed miracles; named among the most-requested banned books in Jordan in 2010. Exact ban year unknown (proxy). Adds a Jordan ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Jordan ban; documented by NPR (citing Foreign Policy).

entries.title: Arab Political Humour
entries.description_ban: A sarcastic survey of the Middle East, its rulers and customs; named among the most-requested banned books in Jordan in 2010. Exact ban year unknown (proxy).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by NPR (citing Foreign Policy).

entries.title: The Madman of Freedom Square
entries.description_ban: The Jordanian Department of Press and Publications told the Arabic publisher in April 2012 that the story collection was 'prohibited from trading' in Jordan, citing foul language and derision of religion.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific DPP ban; documented by ArabLit quoting the DPP notice.

entries.title: The Testaments of the Murdered
entries.description_ban: Analysis of letters attributed to Saddam Hussein and US decision-making before the 2003 invasion; the DPP pulled it from circulation citing harm to Jordan's ties with Iraq and referred it to court.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific DPP withdrawal; documented by Ammon News (medium confidence).

entries.title: The Witch Doctor of Umm Suqeim
entries.description_ban: Short-story collection set in Dubai; every copy was confiscated from Kinokuniya Dubai by the National Media Council over depictions of homosexuality, prostitution and abused domestic workers, and returned to the UK wholesaler.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific confiscation; documented by Publishing Perspectives.

entries.title: Dubai: The Vulnerability of Success
entries.description_ban: Academic study of Dubai; the author and publisher said it was blacklisted by the National Media Council with distributors told not to supply it. The NMC denied a formal ban and later authorised sale amid press coverage.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific blacklisting (disputed); documented by The National (medium confidence).

entries.title: Desperate in Dubai
entries.description_ban: Novel about an Emirati woman's double life and taboo themes of sexuality and extramarital relationships; reported by The National as 'banned from and reintroduced to' UAE bookstores.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific retail removal (later reversed); documented by The National (medium confidence).

entries.title: Love Comes Later
entries.description_ban: First English-language novel set in Qatar; rejected by the Ministry of Culture for retail sale, reportedly 'because it was about Qatar and Qataris'.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Ministry of Culture ban; documented by Doha News.

entries.title: Snow White (Disney illustrated edition)
entries.description_ban: SEK International School in Doha pulled the illustrated Snow White from its library after a parent complaint; Qatar's Supreme Education Council ordered the removal over illustrations and phrases deemed indecent.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific school-library removal ordered by the Supreme Education Council; documented by CBLDF and Times of Israel (medium confidence).
